description: The figure is understood to be a multi-panel composition concerning Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels, though the scientific image pixels are unreadable and only a non-informative placeholder is visible. Based on the provided textual summary, the panels likely present engineering stress–strain curves and corresponding work-hardening rate plots for alloy series including Fe30Mn11Al(0.6–1.5)C, Fe(20–35)Mn11Al1C, and Fe30Mn(6–12)Al1C after solution treatment at 1050 °C or 1100 °C for 2 h with water quenching. The context reports that yield strength increases markedly with carbon content (from 542 MPa at 0.6 %C to 1125 MPa at 1.5 %C) while total elongation decreases from 74 % to 44 %, accompanied by κ-carbide coarsening from ~1 nm to ~50 nm. Additional panels may display EBSD phase maps or TEM dark-field images revealing austenite grain sizes, BCC ferrite fractions, and coherent κ-carbide distributions. The core conclusion supported is that alloying content governs the trade-off between strength and ductility via microstructural features such as κ-carbide precipitation and ferrite fraction.

Figure 1 presents the engineering drawings defining the geometries of the mechanical test specimens machined from solution-treated Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel rods. Subfigure (a) depicts the cylindrical tensile specimen featuring M12 threaded gripping ends, R5 transition fillets, a Ø5 mm gauge section, and a 30 mm gauge length. Subfigure (b) shows the standard Charpy V-notch impact specimen with a 10 × 10 mm cross-section, 55 mm total length, and a 2 mm deep V-notch. According to the experimental description, the tensile specimen was tested at ambient temperature with an initial strain rate of 1.1 × 10^-3 s^-1, while the Charpy specimen was used for impact toughness measurements at −40 °C with a 500 J MTS impact tester. These standardized configurations ensure comparability across all investigated alloy compositions and support the consistent evaluation of tensile properties and sub-zero impact energy.

This figure presents room-temperature engineering stress-strain curves (σ_E versus ε_E) for Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels after solution treatment, showing panels (a) and (b). Panel (a) displays curves for Fe30Mn11AlxC steels solution-treated at 1050°C for 2 h with carbon contents of 0.6C (black), 1.0C (red), 1.2C (green), and 1.5C (blue). Increasing carbon from 0.6% to 1.5% raises the yield strength from approximately 542 MPa to 1125 MPa but reduces tensile elongation from about 60% to 20%, accompanied by a transition from pronounced work hardening to work softening. Panel (b) shows curves for FexMn11Al1C steels under the same treatment with manganese contents of 20Mn (black), 25Mn (red), 30Mn (green), and 34Mn (blue); increasing Mn from 20% to 34% decreases the yield strength from about 896 MPa to 401 MPa while increasing total elongation from 28.5% to 60% and enhancing the work-hardening rate. The caption states that the complete Fig. 2 additionally includes panels (c) and (d) examining Al-content effects at 1050°C and 1100°C, respectively. Note that post-yield stress-strain jumps are caused by extensometer removal and do not affect mechanical measurements. These results demonstrate that C and Al additions strengthen the steel at the expense of ductility, whereas Mn enhances ductility but reduces strength, revealing key compositional trade-offs for property optimization.

The figure displays engineering stress-strain (σ_E–ε_E) curves for Fe30MnxAl1C low-density steels under solution treatment at 1050°C (c) and 1100°C (d), with aluminum contents of 6Al, 8Al, 10Al, 11Al, and 12Al (wt%). The 6Al and 8Al alloys exhibit lower yield strengths (~400–550 MPa) but superior ductility exceeding 70% engineering strain. As Al increases to 11Al, the yield strength rises markedly while ductility remains above 60%. In contrast, the 12Al alloy shows significantly reduced tensile ductility, fracturing at approximately 40–50% strain at 1050°C and slightly higher at 1100°C, despite its higher flow stress. Raising the solution temperature from 1050°C to 1100°C marginally lowers the yield strength for most compositions. These results indicate that Al addition strengthens austenite but causes a sharp ductility loss beyond 11Al, consistent with microstructural evolution involving κ-carbide precipitation and minor ferrite formation at higher Al contents.

Figure 3 shows the microstructure of Fe30Mn11Al(x)C low-density steels with carbon contents of 0.6%, 1.0%, 1.2%, and 1.5% after solution treatment at 1050°C for 2 h. Subfigures (a)-(d) are EBSD maps (scale bar 200 μm) displaying grain boundaries with misorientation >15° (black), Σ3 twin boundaries (red), and BCC ferrite phase (green). The 0.6%C alloy (a) exhibits a duplex microstructure of FCC austenite and about 30% BCC ferrite, while the 1.0%C (b), 1.2%C (c), and 1.5%C (d) alloys are single-phase austenite with coarser grains. The fractional twin boundary length decreases from nearly 60% at 0.6%C to 50% at 1.5%C. Subfigures (e)-(h) are dark-field TEM images (scale bars 20 nm for e-f and 100 nm for g-h) viewed along the [001]γ zone axis using κ-carbide diffraction spots, revealing uniformly distributed nano-sized κ-carbides whose size increases with carbon content: <1 nm at 0.6%C (e), ~2 nm at 1.0%C (f), ~25 nm at 1.2%C (g), and ~80 nm with regular rectangular morphology at 1.5%C (h). Inset SAED patterns confirm the (001)κ || (001)γ orientation relationship. These observations collectively demonstrate that increasing carbon content suppresses ferrite formation, coarsens austenite grains, reduces twin boundary fraction, and markedly promotes κ-carbide coarsening.

Figure 4 presents the microstructure of Fe(x)Mn11Al1C low-density steels with Mn contents of 20%, 25%, 30%, and 35%. Subfigures (a)–(d) are EBSD boundary maps (200 μm scale bars) displaying austenite grains, high-angle grain boundaries (black lines, θ > 15°), Σ3 twin boundaries (red lines), and BCC ferrite regions (green). The 20%Mn sample (a) exhibits duplex austenite plus ferrite; as Mn increases to 25% (b), 30% (c), and 35% (d), the BCC phase disappears and the austenite grain size coarsens. Citation sentences report austenite grain sizes of approximately 9.9 μm, 18.8 μm, 23.7 μm, and 33 μm, respectively, with twin boundary fractions around 60–62%. Subfigures (e)–(h) are dark-field TEM images (scale bars 100 nm for e–f and 20 nm for g–h) revealing nanoscale κ-carbide precipitates as bright contrast within the austenite matrix. These images were acquired along the [001]γ zone axis using κ-carbide diffraction spots indicated in the inset SAED patterns. The average κ-carbide size decreases markedly with increasing Mn content, from about 18 nm in the 20%Mn sample (e) to roughly 5 nm (f), 3 nm (g), and 2 nm (h) for 25%, 30%, and 35% Mn, respectively, as reported in the text. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that increasing Mn content suppresses ferrite, coarsens austenite grains while maintaining a high twin-boundary fraction, and substantially refines κ-carbides, establishing the microstructural basis for composition-dependent mechanical properties.

Figure 5 presents the microstructure of Fe30Mn(x)Al1C low-density steels with Al contents of 8%, 10%, 11%, and 12%. Subfigures (a–d) are EBSD maps showing austenite grain boundaries (black, θ>15°), annealing twin boundaries (red, Σ3), and BCC ferrite phase (green in d), with 200 μm scale bars. Caption and associated text report average grain sizes of approximately 28.3 μm, 29.7 μm, and 23.7 μm for 8%, 10%, and 11% Al, respectively, while the 12% Al steel exhibits significant refinement to about 12.6 μm due to the presence of ~3.1 vol.% ferrite. Subfigure (e) is a bright-field TEM image (20 nm scale bar) of the 8% Al sample with an inset SAED pattern along the [001]γ zone axis, showing an absence of κ-carbide precipitation. Subfigures (f–h) are dark-field TEM images of the 10%, 11%, and 12% Al samples (scale bars 20 nm for f–g and 100 nm for h), taken using κ-carbide diffraction spots; these reveal extensive κ-carbide formation with average diameters reported as ~1 nm, ~1.5 nm, and ~15 nm, respectively. The overall figure supports the conclusion that increasing Al content strongly promotes κ-carbide precipitation and, at 12% Al, induces ferrite formation that refines the austenite grain structure.

Figure 6 quantifies the alloying effects on microstructure and density of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels after solution treatment at 1050°C for 2 h and water quenching. Panel (a) plots average κ-carbide diameter (d_Car, nm) against element content (mass %), where stars represent Al%, blue circles represent Mn%, and open red squares represent 10×C% to allow common-axis comparison. The visual data and caption state that C and Al strongly accelerate κ-carbide coarsening: a C increase from ~0.6 % to 1.5 % enlarges d_Car sharply from ~1 nm to ~50 nm, while an Al increase from ~8 % to 12 % raises size from ~0.5 nm to ~15 nm. Conversely, Mn shows only a weak inverse effect, with d_Car decreasing from ~5 nm to ~2 nm as Mn rises from ~25 % to 35 %. Panel (b) plots mass density (ρ, g/cm³) versus alloy content, with linear fits indicating density decreases monotonically with each solute; the fitted equation reads ρ = 8.1 – 0.102C% – 0.1Al% – 0.01Mn%. Together, these panels establish that C and Al are the principal microstructural controllers of κ-carbide size, whereas all three elements linearly reduce bulk density.

This figure presents the dependence of mechanical properties on κ-carbide size (d_Car) in solution-treated Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels with varying alloying contents. Panel (a) shows that both yield strength (red circles, R_p0.2 = 478 + 97.1 d_Car^0.5) and tensile strength (black squares, R_m = 786 + 54.6 d_Car^0.5) increase linearly with the square root of κ-carbide size. Panel (b) and (c) show that total tensile elongation (A_5 = 64.5 - 6.445 d_Car^0.5) and area reduction (Z = 78 - 3.85 d_Car^0.5) decrease linearly with d_Car^0.5, respectively. Panel (d) plots Charpy impact toughness at -40°C (Akv) versus d_Car^0.5, revealing a severe drop with increasing carbide size; the dashed linear fit is applied only for d_Car^0.5 < 2. These relationships indicate that strength, ductility, and toughness are primarily governed by κ-carbide size through a shear strengthening mechanism, rather than by grain size or ferrite volume fraction, supporting alloy design strategies that tune precipitate size via composition to achieve high strength-ductility balance.

Figure 8 presents the work hardening behavior of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels, showing true tensile stress-strain curves (solid lines) and corresponding work hardening (WH) rate curves (dashed lines) as functions of true tensile strain (ε_T). Panel (a) compares steels with varying carbon content (0.6C, 1.0C, 1.2C, 1.5C) after solution treatment at 1050 °C, where the WH rate increases with decreasing carbon content, reaching a maximum of approximately 1900 MPa for the 0.6%C steel. Panel (b) shows the effect of Mn content (20Mn, 25Mn, 30Mn, 35Mn) at 1050 °C, with the WH rate increasing up to about 2300 MPa for 35%Mn. Panels (c) and (d) display the effect of Al content (6Al–12Al) after solution treatment at 1050 °C and 1100 °C, respectively; the WH rate increases with decreasing Al content, with a maximum near 2100 MPa for 6%Al at 1050 °C. The caption notes that the ultimate true stress (σ_TS ≈ 1350 MPa) and fracture true stress (σ_FS ≈ 2150 MPa) remain nearly constant regardless of alloying variations. The steels with 0.6C and 30Mn exhibit a monotonic decrease in WH rate, whereas other compositions show a characteristic rapid decline followed by a slow rise and subsequent second decline. These trends support the conclusion that high ductility correlates with high work hardening capacity and that κ-carbide size governs work hardening behavior through a shear strengthening mechanism.
